meta.title: Observability Without the Budget: Prometheus + Grafana for Small Teams
meta.description: A practical guide to setting up production monitoring with Prometheus and Grafana for small teams without overspending.
meta.ogTitle: Observability Without the Budget: Prometheus + Grafana for Small Teams
meta.ogDescription: A practical guide to setting up production monitoring with Prometheus and Grafana for small teams.

content:
Enterprise observability tools (Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace) are powerful, but their costs can skyrocket quickly for small teams. A team with 5 microservices can pay $500-$2000/month just for monitoring. The open source alternative — Prometheus + Grafana — offers the same level of observability for a fraction of the cost. In this guide I'll show you how to set it up in production without overspending.

Why Prometheus + Grafana?

Prometheus is a time-series metrics system with a multidimensional data model. Grafana is the visualization layer that connects to Prometheus (and dozens of other sources). Together they cover:

  • Infrastructure metrics: CPU, RAM, disk, network (via node_exporter)
  • Application metrics: latency, error rate, throughput (via client libraries)
  • Alerts: integration with Slack, PagerDuty, email
  • Dashboards: real-time visualization with reusable panels

Estimated cost: a $15-30/month VM (2 CPU, 4GB RAM, 50GB SSD) can run Prometheus + Grafana for a small team. No per-host costs, no per-metric costs, no artificial limits.

Recommended architecture

For small teams, a simple architecture is more than enough:

  • 1 Prometheus server (can share a VM with Grafana)
  • Node Exporter on each monitored server
  • Grafana with preloaded dashboards
  • Alertmanager for notifications

Installation with Docker Compose

The fastest way to set everything up is with Docker Compose. This complete stack gives you monitoring in under 10 minutes:

# docker-compose.yml
version: "3.8"

services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:v2.53.0
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
      - prometheus_data:/prometheus
    command:
      - "--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml"
      - "--storage.tsdb.retention.time=30d"
      - "--storage.tsdb.retention.size=10GB"
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    restart: unless-stopped

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:11.1.0
    volumes:
      - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./grafana/provisioning:/etc/grafana/provisioning
    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=changeme
      - GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS=grafana-piechart-panel
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - prometheus

  alertmanager:
    image: prom/alertmanager:v0.27.0
    volumes:
      - ./alertmanager.yml:/etc/alertmanager/alertmanager.yml
    ports:
      - "9093:9093"
    restart: unless-stopped

  node_exporter:
    image: prom/node-exporter:v1.8.1
    network_mode: host
    pid: host
    volumes:
      - /:/host:ro,rslave
    command:
      - "--path.rootfs=/host"
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  prometheus_data:
  grafana_data:

Prometheus configuration

# prometheus.yml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

alerting:
  alertmanagers:
    - static_configs:
        - targets: ["alertmanager:9093"]

rule_files:
  - "alerts/*.yml"

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]

  - job_name: "node"
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - "host1:9100"
          - "host2:9100"
          - "host3:9100"

  - job_name: "api"
    metrics_path: "/metrics"
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - "api1.example.com:8080"
          - "api2.example.com:8080"

Useful and affordable alerts

Alerts cost nothing. Here are the essential rules for any small team:

# alerts/infra.yml
groups:
  - name: infra
    rules:
      - alert: HighCPUUsage
        expr: 100 - (avg by(instance) (rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{
          mode="idle"}[5m])) * 100) > 80
        for: 10m
        annotations:
          summary: "CPU > 80% en {{ $labels.instance }}"

      - alert: DiskSpaceLow
        expr: (node_filesystem_avail_bytes{
          mountpoint="/",
          fstype!~"tmpfs|overlay"} / node_filesystem_size_bytes{
          mountpoint="/",
          fstype!~"tmpfs|overlay"}) * 100 < 15
        for: 5m
        annotations:
          summary: "Disco < 15% libre en {{ $labels.instance }}"

      - alert: InstanceDown
        expr: up == 0
        for: 2m
        annotations:
          summary: "Instancia {{ $labels.instance }} caída"

      - alert: HighMemoryUsage
        expr: (1 - node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes /
          node_memory_MemTotal_bytes) * 100 > 90
        for: 10m
        annotations:
          summary: "RAM > 90% en {{ $labels.instance }}"

Alertmanager: Slack notifications

# alertmanager.yml
route:
  receiver: "slack"
  group_by: ["alertname", "instance"]
  group_wait: 30s
  group_interval: 5m
  repeat_interval: 4h

receivers:
  - name: "slack"
    slack_configs:
      - api_url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/YOUR/WEBHOOK/URL"
        channel: "#alerts"
        title: '{{ .GroupLabels.alertname }}'
        text: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.summary }}'
        send_resolved: true

Dashboards: don't build them, import them

Grafana has a catalog of community dashboards at grafana.com/dashboards. The most useful for small teams:

  • Node Exporter Full (ID: 1860) — complete server metrics
  • Prometheus 2.0 Stats (ID: 3662) — Prometheus health itself
  • Docker Monitoring (ID: 10619) — Docker containers

To provision them automatically, use Grafana's provisioning directory:

# grafana/provisioning/dashboards/dashboards.yml
apiVersion: 1

providers:
  - name: "default"
    orgId: 1
    folder: ""
    type: file
    disableDeletion: false
    updateIntervalSeconds: 10
    options:
      path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards

Application metrics with Go

To expose metrics from your Go application, use the official client library:

package main

import (
  "net/http"
  "github.com/prometheus/client_golang/prometheus"
  "github.com/prometheus/client_golang/prometheus/promhttp"
)

var (
  httpRequestsTotal = prometheus.NewCounterVec(
    prometheus.CounterOpts{
      Name: "http_requests_total",
      Help: "Total de requests HTTP",
    },
    []string{"method", "endpoint", "status"},
  )

  httpRequestDuration = prometheus.NewHistogramVec(
    prometheus.HistogramOpts{
      Name:    "http_request_duration_seconds",
      Help:    "Duración de requests HTTP",
      Buckets: prometheus.DefBuckets,
    },
    []string{"method", "endpoint"},
  )
)

func init() {
  prometheus.MustRegister(httpRequestsTotal)
  prometheus.MustRegister(httpRequestDuration)
}

func main() {
  http.Handle("/metrics", promhttp.Handler())
  http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)
}

Best practices to avoid cost spikes

  • Data retention: 30 days is enough for small teams. Adjust --storage.tsdb.retention.time
  • Scrape interval: 15s for production, 30s-60s for staging. Each scrape has a cost in IO and CPU
  • Cardinal metrics: avoid labels with unique values per request (user_id, session_id). This inflates the database exponentially
  • Backup: back up only Grafana dashboards (JSON) and configuration, not historical metrics

The cardinality trap: a label like user_id with 10,000 users creates 10,000 unique time series per metric. With 20 metrics, that's 200,000 series. Prometheus can handle it, but performance degrades and storage grows uncontrollably.

Conclusion

Prometheus + Grafana is not a "poor man's" solution. It's a solid, battle-tested option used by companies of all sizes. For small teams, the savings are significant and the functionality is on par with enterprise solutions. With an initial investment of $15-30/month in infrastructure and an afternoon of setup, you get a professional monitoring system that scales with your team.
